Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed intransitive clause with possessed noun phrase; metaphorical body-part/landscape image.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To remain firm and calm despite pressure, like a canoe held steady near stones.
Usage Contexts: daily speech; advice; family; leadership; river travel
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Upper Rio Negro river imagery; stones and current are common reference points for patience and steadiness.
First Example Sentence: Yande ramuĩ umunhã puçanga nheenga, ma ta iakwá upitá itá suíri.
Second Example Sentence: Kuximawara mira ukuntai siya, asuí Maria iakwá upitá itá suíri.
Third Example Sentence: Tiwá yane rembiapo uikú pukú, yasu yapitá itá suíri.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed clause with subject noun and motion verb; hyperbolic metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: News or gossip spreads very quickly and far.
Usage Contexts: community news; humor; politics; social media
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Echoes oral circulation of news along rivers and villages; modern use can fit radio or phone messages.
First Example Sentence: Aintá ukupirũ yepé nheenga suiwara, asuí nheenga usasá iwaka rupí.
Second Example Sentence: Reunião resé marandua usasá iwaka rupí, ma ape nemaã resé.
Third Example Sentence: Akangaisima nheenga usasá iwaka rupí mairamé uikú rori reté.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Verbal phrase with reflexive/active predicate and object; metaphorical premature action.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To act too early, before the right time or evidence appears.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on fishing knowledge: taking the fish before it is properly within reach ruins the catch.
First Example Sentence: Ti uyuká pira senundé pupé; yarikuté yamaã mamé aé uwiké.
Second Example Sentence: Paulo uyuká pira senundé pupé, upurungitá yepé marandua ti supí.
Third Example Sentence: Mairamé yanepytasú resé, ti yasasá yepé renda uyuká pira senundé pupé.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Possessive existential clause; figurative predicate with celestial noun.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person is lucky, cheerful, favored, or brings good energy.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Aé uikú rori asuí puranga reté.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Kurasí ‘sun’ is a central Amazonian image of warmth, clarity, and life.
First Example Sentence: Kunhãtaĩ uyurí ramé, yande ruka upitá rori: aé uriku kurasí ipó pupé.
Second Example Sentence: Se irũ unheengari puranga; aé uriku kurasí ipó pupé.
Third Example Sentence: Tupana ara upé, amunhã se rembiapo, ma aikué kurasí se pó pupé.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Transitive desiderative clause with object; impossible-desire metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To want too much or demand something impossible.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Moon imagery marks desire beyond human reach; used for exaggeration and teasing.
First Example Sentence: Taina uputari yasí mokõi pó pupé: yepé ara pupé, aé uputari upá maã.
Second Example Sentence: Yandé ti yapuderi umunhã kwa rembiapo se suí; ne reputari yasí mokõi pó pupé.
Third Example Sentence: Mairamé karai uputari pirá, maniaka, asuí arama mirá yepé rese, aé uputari yasí mokõi pó pupé.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominal subject with locative predicate; metaphor of a head lost in forest-thought.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To be confused, distracted, or mentally lost.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: References forest paths and the risk of losing direction without attention or guidance.
First Example Sentence: Se akanga upitá kaá piterupé, ma professora umuyekwa yande raperã.
Second Example Sentence: Ne remundú se suí maã resé? Se akanga upitá kaá piterupé kuri.
Third Example Sentence: Aé usú mercado upé, ma iakanga upitá kaá piterupé asuí ti umbeú maã uputari.

Idiom (Nheengatu): Nheenga urikú mira rupí pirá-ĩa suí
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Complex existential clause with comparative/source phrase; metaphor of a word carrying fish-bone.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A comment sounds small but has a sharp hidden criticism.
Usage Contexts: sarcasm; politics; family tension; literature; public speech
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Fish bones evoke something tiny yet painful, common in riverine eating practices and joking warnings.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Nheengatu): /ɲeˈẽɡa uɾiˈku miˈɾa ɾuˈpi piˈɾa ˈĩa suˈi/
First Example Sentence: Karai nheenga urikú mira rupí pirá-ĩa suí, asuí upá mira ukunumã.
Second Example Sentence: Kunhã umbeú puranga, ma inheenga urikú pirá-ĩa suí.
Third Example Sentence: Ti remuari; se nheenga urikú mira rupí pirá-ĩa suí resewara.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Reflexive/transitive construction with relative clause; self-fashioning metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To pretend to be important or grander than one really is.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Contrasts the small quick agouti with the large confident tapir; Amazonian animal imagery marks social irony.
First Example Sentence: Aé uriku yepé papel mirĩ, ma uyumunhã tawatá waá akutiara upé.
Second Example Sentence: Ti resarái: mirĩ rembiapo yuiri uikú puranga; ti reyumunhã tawatá waá akutiara upé.
Third Example Sentence: Reunião upé, Pedro uyumunhã tawatá waá akutiara upé asuí upurungitá siya.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Biclausal temporal construction with personified natural subjects; proverbial structure.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: When warning signs appear, attentive people should listen carefully.
Usage Contexts: environment; elders’ advice; leadership; safety; storytelling
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects attentive listening to forest and water as practical knowledge in river communities.
First Example Sentence: Mairamé igarapé unheenga, kaá uyapysaka; yande yuiri yayapysaka arã.
Second Example Sentence: Aintá usemu senundé, ma waimĩ umbeú: mairamé igarapé unheenga, kaá uyapysaka.
Third Example Sentence: Yane ruka resé marandua ti puranga, asuí paya umbeú kwa nheenga.

Idiom (Nheengatu): Upitá yepé iwí mokõi sapó pupé
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Locative existential clause with numeral noun phrase; root metaphor for divided belonging.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To belong to two worlds, communities, or responsibilities at once.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Evokes roots in soil and the multilingual reality of Rio Negro peoples moving among Nheengatu, Portuguese, and Indigenous languages.
First Example Sentence: Se membira upurungitá nheengatu asuí português; aé upitá yepé iwí mokõi sapó pupé.
Second Example Sentence: Mairamé asú cidade upé, se pyá upitá yepé iwí mokõi sapó pupé.
Third Example Sentence: Professor umbeú: yande nheenga umuyekwa yande sapó, ma yande rape usú apekatú.
